editorialNotes: Every connection must have >=1 source with tier="primary" (gov.uk/parliament.uk/court record). Connections with liveProceedings=true render a cautionary banner and must quote only official findings in the public record. Person IDs are kebab-case (surname-given for individuals; firm-<slug> for firms). Each person record carries a kind field: "person" (individual political figure or civil servant) or "firm" (company subject — used for donor-contractor pattern records). Counterparty IDs match the canonical IDs used elsewhere in Gracchus; adjacent_firm entries carry no ID.

connections.summary: Owen Paterson was paid approximately £100,000 a year as a consultant to Randox Laboratories while sitting as an MP, and was found by the Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards to have committed an 'egregious' breach of paid-advocacy rules by lobbying for the company inside government.
connections.detail:
Paterson took up a paid consultancy with Randox Laboratories in August 2015, reported at c. £100,000 per year, alongside a separate paid role with Lynn's Country Foods. During his period of engagement Randox received more than £500m of UK government COVID testing contracts, including a £133m contract in March 2020 for testing kits and lab processing and a £346m extension awarded later that year.

In October 2021 the Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards and the Commons Committee on Standards concluded that Paterson had 'repeatedly used his privileged position to benefit two companies for whom he was a paid consultant' and had committed 'an egregious case of paid advocacy'. The Committee recommended a 30-day suspension. The Government's attempt to replace the standards process with a new committee (the Leadsom amendment) passed a whipped Commons vote on 3 November 2021 and was abandoned within 24 hours after cross-party opposition. Paterson resigned as MP for North Shropshire on 5 November 2021.

DHSC later released WhatsApp and email exchanges between Paterson and the then-Health Secretary Matt Hancock regarding Randox contracts, disclosed under FOI and reported by Byline Times in February 2022. No criminal proceedings followed; the matter is treated as closed at the regulatory level on the Commissioner's finding.

financial.relatedContractsDescription: £500m+ in Randox COVID testing contracts

regulatoryFindings.body: Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards / Committee on Standards
regulatoryFindings.quotedText: No previous case of paid advocacy has seen so many breaches or such a clear pattern of behaviour in failing to separate private and public interests. Mr Paterson's actions were an egregious case of paid advocacy.
regulatoryFindings.outcome: Committee on Standards recommended a 30-day suspension; Paterson resigned as MP on 5 November 2021.

connections.summary: Baroness Mone referred PPE Medpro into the government's 'VIP / high-priority' procurement lane, after which the firm won c. £203m of DHSC PPE contracts; the High Court has ordered PPE Medpro to repay c. £122m, and a National Crime Agency criminal investigation into her and her husband Doug Barrowman remains active.
connections.detail:
PPE Medpro Ltd was incorporated in May 2020 and awarded two DHSC contracts totalling a reported £203m for face masks and sterile surgical gowns, entered through the 'VIP / high-priority lane' after a referral by Baroness Mone. For more than two years Mone and Doug Barrowman publicly denied any involvement with the firm; they later confirmed, in a BBC interview broadcast in December 2023, that Barrowman was the ultimate beneficial owner. Reporting indicates Barrowman received more than £60m in profits, with £29m transferred to a trust benefiting Mone and her children.

In 2024 the High Court ruled in favour of DHSC in its civil claim against PPE Medpro, ordering the firm to repay c. £122m for surgical gowns later deemed unsuitable for use. That judgment is a closed finding in the public record.

Separately, the National Crime Agency opened a criminal investigation in May 2021 and executed search warrants at Mone and Barrowman's homes and at PPE Medpro offices in April 2022; as of early 2026 that investigation is ongoing. The UK Covid-19 Inquiry declined a late bid by Mone to participate as a core participant in Module 5 (procurement). Mone took leave of absence from the House of Lords in December 2022. Gracchus reports only findings already entered into the public record.

connections.summary: Akshata Murty, wife of Rishi Sunak, holds a reported 0.94% stake (c. 39m shares) in Infosys — a UK government IT supplier that has received at least £47m of public contracts since Sunak became Chancellor in February 2020.
connections.detail:
Infosys was co-founded by N. R. Narayana Murty, father of Akshata Murty. Her shareholding in Infosys has been publicly reported at approximately 39m shares (c. 0.94% of the company, valued at around £610m at time of reporting). Rishi Sunak became Chancellor of the Exchequer in February 2020 and served as Prime Minister from October 2022 to July 2024.

Per reporting by Democracy for Sale, Full Fact and LBC, Infosys has received at least £66m in UK public-sector contracts since 2015, with more than £47m of that awarded after Sunak's appointment as Chancellor. A c.50% year-on-year rise in Infosys's UK public-sector invoices was reported for 2023.

The Independent Adviser on Ministers' Interests ruled that Sunak did not need to declare his wife's Infosys stake on parliamentary registers, and the Cabinet Office has stated that neither Sunak nor his ministers were involved in Infosys contract-award decisions. No regulator, court or parliamentary committee has made any finding of wrongdoing against Sunak in relation to Infosys. A separate 2022 controversy concerning Akshata Murty's non-domiciled tax status ended with her voluntarily giving up non-dom status in April 2022.

Gracchus records this connection as a matter of public fact: the shareholding exists, the contracts exist, the timing overlaps with Sunak's tenure. Gracchus does not assert any causal link between the two.

connections.summary: David Cameron took a part-time advisory role at Greensill Capital in 2018, more than two years after leaving Downing Street, and in 2020 lobbied HM Treasury and DHSC ministers and officials on Greensill's behalf; the Commons Treasury Committee concluded he showed 'a significant lack of judgement' but had not broken the rules, because the two-year ACOBA cooling-off period had already expired.
connections.detail:
Cameron resigned as Prime Minister in July 2016 and took up a part-time advisory role with Greensill Capital in August 2018 - more than two years after leaving office. Because more than two years had elapsed between his resignation and the appointment, ACOBA's business-appointment rules did not apply and no ACOBA advice was sought or required. Reporting indicates he held share options in Greensill with a nominal value in the tens of millions of pounds.

During the COVID-19 pandemic in spring 2020 Cameron lobbied HM Treasury, including the then-Chancellor Rishi Sunak, and DHSC for Greensill to be granted access to the Covid Corporate Financing Facility (CCFF). The requests were declined. Text messages, emails and call records were later disclosed under pressure from the Treasury Committee.

The Commons Treasury Committee's July 2021 report Lessons from Greensill Capital concluded that Cameron had not broken lobbying rules as they stood, but that the rules had 'insufficient strength' and that his use of informal lobbying routes showed 'a significant lack of judgement'. The Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee and the Boardman Review reached related findings on the wider governance picture.

Greensill Capital is not among Gracchus's 116 core tracker projects; the lobbying target was HM Treasury's CCFF scheme and DHSC, both tracked departments. Gracchus carries this as an adjacent-firm connection.

counterparty.note: Not a Gracchus-tracked prime supplier. The lobbying targeted HM Treasury (CCFF) and DHSC - both tracked departments. Carried as adjacent_firm.

regulatoryFindings.outcome: Committee concluded Cameron had not broken lobbying rules, but that the rules themselves had 'insufficient strength'.

connections.summary: Bill Crothers, the Government's Chief Commercial Officer at the Cabinet Office, took up a paid advisory role with Greensill Capital in September 2015 while still a serving civil servant, without declaring the arrangement to the civil service ethics arrangements or seeking ACOBA advice on his post-Crown-service plans; PACAC later found 'too little consideration appears to have been paid to the potential reputational risk'.
connections.detail:
Crothers joined the Cabinet Office as Government Chief Commercial Officer in 2012, with a salary reported up to c. £149,000 per year, and was closely involved in bringing Lex Greensill into government as a 'crown representative'. In September 2015, while still employed in the civil service, Crothers took up a paid advisory role with Greensill Capital. He left the civil service in November 2015 and became a director of Greensill Capital in August 2016.

The Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee's report Propriety of Governance in Light of Greensill found that Crothers had not declared the Greensill role to civil-service ethics arrangements at the time, and had not sought ACOBA's advice on his post-Crown-service role. The Committee concluded that there were 'no obvious conflicts' at the point he was hired by Greensill - because Greensill had limited UK public-sector contracts at that time - but that 'too little consideration appears to have been paid to the potential reputational risk'.

Crothers's Crown Commercial Service role had oversight of the supplier base tracked by Gracchus (Serco, Capita, Atos, Fujitsu, Mitie, G4S and others). The revelation of his dual employment triggered the Boardman Review of civil-service dual employment. Greensill Capital collapsed in March 2021. Gracchus carries this as an adjacent-firm connection.

connections.summary: Alex Bourne, the former landlord of Matt Hancock's local pub in the Health Secretary's West Suffolk constituency, secured a contract through his plastics firm Hinpack to supply specimen collection tubes and funnels for NHS COVID-19 testing - a reported ~£30m of DHSC-funded work - after contacting Hancock directly via WhatsApp; Hinpack had no prior PPE or diagnostics manufacturing record.
connections.detail:
Alex Bourne was the landlord of the Cock Inn in Little Thurlow, in Matt Hancock's West Suffolk constituency. In spring 2020 Bourne contacted Hancock via WhatsApp to offer his plastics firm Hinpack's services for the pandemic response. Hinpack had no prior record of manufacturing medical or diagnostic products.

Hinpack went on to partner with a diagnostics supplier to produce specimen collection tubes and funnels for COVID-19 testing, in work that was reported by the Guardian and Good Law Project as amounting to roughly £30m of DHSC-funded contract value. The contract was not awarded through open competitive tender.

Both Hancock and Bourne have publicly stated that Hancock was not personally involved in awarding the contract, and that Hancock told Bourne to apply through the Government's online PPE portal. No regulator, court or parliamentary committee has made a finding of rule-breaking by either individual. Gracchus records this connection because of the public-interest pattern - a direct personal approach to a Secretary of State leading to procurement access - and not because of any proven wrongdoing. DHSC is the tracked buyer; Hinpack is carried as an adjacent firm.

connections.summary: Geoffrey Cox earned more than £6m from outside legal work during his time as Conservative MP for Torridge and West Devon - including more than £800,000 from Withers LLP for advising the Government of the British Virgin Islands in an inquiry set up by the UK Foreign Office to investigate alleged corruption in the BVI - all declared in the Register of Members' Financial Interests.
connections.detail:
Cox has practised at the Bar throughout his time as an MP, most prominently through Withers LLP. His outside earnings, declared in the Register of Members' Financial Interests, have consistently placed him among the highest-earning MPs of his era. Reporting by ITV News, The Week and Byline Times, drawing on the Register, put his outside earnings since the 2019 general election at more than £2m; wider reporting on his earnings across his time in Parliament places the total above £6m.

His largest single engagement has been advising the Government of the British Virgin Islands in connection with an inquiry set up by the UK Foreign Office to investigate alleged corruption in the BVI. Withers LLP's engagement with the BVI government was reported at a rate of £468,000 per year for 48 hours of work per month, with more than £150,000 specifically for BVI inquiry work and more than £800,000 in aggregate from Withers LLP over the period.

In 2016 the Standards Committee found Cox had committed a 'serious breach' of Commons rules by failing to register c. £400,000 of outside earnings (eleven payments) within the 28-day window. No subsequent breach finding has been made against Cox in relation to the BVI work specifically, and no finding of paid advocacy has been made. Cox maintains that the work does not breach any rules.

Gracchus records this connection as a matter of public fact: the earnings exist, they are declared in the Register, and they include work for a jurisdiction that was the subject of a UK-government-initiated inquiry. Gracchus does not assert any causal link to tracked Gracchus suppliers - there is none - and the record is carried as adjacent.

connections.summary: George Osborne joined BlackRock Investment Institute as a senior adviser in February 2017, reported at c. £650,000 a year for one day a week, less than seven months after leaving the Treasury; ACOBA approved the appointment subject to conditions.
connections.detail:
Osborne resigned as Chancellor in July 2016 and was appointed senior adviser to the BlackRock Investment Institute in February 2017, on terms reported at approximately £650,000 per year for one day's work per week. He held the role concurrently with the editorship of the Evening Standard (2017-2020) and, until his resignation as MP for Tatton in April 2017, with his seat in the Commons. He has subsequently taken further private-sector engagements including an advisory role with Coinbase and roles with Robey Warshaw, Santander and the Hoover Institution.

The Advisory Committee on Business Appointments (ACOBA) approved the BlackRock appointment subject to conditions including a lobbying ban. ACOBA's letter recorded that during Osborne's time as Chancellor 'there were no specific policy decisions ... that would have affected BlackRock', and that the Permanent Secretary to HM Treasury had 'no concerns' about the move. The Committee nonetheless attached the standard cooling-off and lobbying restrictions.

No regulator or court has made any finding of rule-breaking. connections.summary: ACOBA found that Philip Hammond had taken an 'unwise step' and acted in a way that was 'not acceptable' when, in July 2020, he emailed an OakNorth Bank proposal directly to the Treasury's second most-senior official within the two-year lobbying-ban window attached to his post-Chancellor adviser role.
connections.detail:
Hammond left office as Chancellor of the Exchequer in July 2019. He took up a paid adviser role with OakNorth Bank in late 2019. As a condition of the appointment, ACOBA had imposed the standard two-year ban on lobbying ministers and officials of the UK government on behalf of OakNorth.

In July 2020 Hammond emailed a proposal from OakNorth - pitching a 'financial toolkit' for the COVID-19 economic response - directly to the Treasury's second most-senior official. ACOBA wrote to Hammond stating that 'there is a reasonable concern that the access you had to a senior Treasury official ... was only made available to OakNorth as a direct result of your time as Chancellor', that the contact represented 'an unwise step', and that his conduct was 'not acceptable'. Hammond is reported to have attempted to block ACOBA from publishing the ruling and to have considered seeking a legal injunction; the Committee conceded the breach was not 'blatant and deliberate'.

OakNorth is not a Gracchus-tracked prime contractor; the connection is to HM Treasury, which sits within the departmental perimeter Gracchus tracks. Standards campaigners (openDemocracy, Spotlight on Corruption) have treated the case as the limit-case of ACOBA's enforcement capability - one of the handful of public ACOBA findings naming a former Cabinet minister as having broken the lobbying rules attached to a post-office appointment.

connections.detail:
Patel resigned as Secretary of State for International Development in November 2017. In 2019, while a backbench MP, she took up a paid strategic adviser role with Viasat Inc., a US-listed satellite-communications firm with UK defence-adjacent interests. The role was reported at a rate of £1,000 per hour. She subsequently returned to government as Home Secretary from July 2019 to September 2022.

The substantive dispute about whether a breach occurred is live as of early 2026; Patel denies any breach.

Viasat is not a Gracchus-tracked prime contractor; it supplies secure satcoms to defence and government customers internationally. Gracchus carries this case in the public record because the Upper Tribunal disclosure ruling is a finding in the public domain, while flagging that the substantive question of breach is unresolved. connections.summary: Laurence Lee, the MoD's former Second Permanent Secretary, took up a paid geo-strategic adviser role with Palantir Technologies UK after leaving the civil service; reporting indicates that while at the MoD he met Palantir executives nine times, accepted hospitality on four occasions, and oversaw negotiations for a reported £25m contract for the company before approaching it for a job shortly after his resignation.
connections.detail:
Laurence Lee served as Second Permanent Secretary at the Ministry of Defence, the second most-senior civil-service position in the department. After leaving the MoD, he took up a paid geo-strategic adviser role with Palantir Technologies UK.

Reporting by Action on Armed Violence and Declassified UK indicates that during his time at the MoD, Lee met Palantir executives on nine occasions, accepted hospitality from the firm on four occasions, and oversaw negotiations for a reported £25m MoD contract for Palantir. He is reported to have approached Palantir for a job shortly after resigning from the civil service. ACOBA approved his post-Crown-service appointment subject to standard conditions including a cooling-off period and lobbying restrictions; no public breach finding has been made against Lee.

Lee's case is the single most documented named example in a wider pattern. Action on Armed Violence's analysis identified more than 120 applications by former senior military officers, civil servants and politicians to take paid positions with firms either holding or bidding for MoD contracts over the last decade. Other named transitions include Admiral Sir Philip Jones (former First Sea Lord, to BAE Systems in 2021), Air Marshal Sir Stuart Atha (to BAE Systems VP Military Operations) and Martyn Williams (former Head of Naval Ships, to BAE Systems Maritime). Transparency International UK, Spotlight on Corruption and the Committee on Standards in Public Life have called the regulatory framework 'not fit for purpose'. Palantir is a Gracchus-tracked supplier and the MoD is a tracked department.

connections.summary: Baroness Dido Harding was appointed by Health Secretary Matt Hancock in May 2020 to lead NHS Test & Trace, a DHSC-budget programme later put at £37bn over two years by the Public Accounts Committee; the High Court ruled in 2022 that her appointment as Chair of the National Institute for Health Protection was unlawful, while her husband - Conservative MP John Penrose - simultaneously served as the Government's Anti-Corruption Champion.
connections.detail:
Baroness Harding was appointed Executive Chair of NHS Test & Trace in May 2020 by Health Secretary Matt Hancock, and Interim Chair of the National Institute for Health Protection in August 2020. The NHS Test & Trace programme's audited costs were put at £37bn over two years by the Commons Public Accounts Committee. Tracked Gracchus suppliers on NHS Test & Trace included Serco, Sitel, Deloitte and Accenture.

In 2022 the High Court ruled, on a judicial review brought by the Good Law Project and the Runnymede Trust, that the appointment of Harding as Chair of the National Institute for Health Protection had been unlawful because the post had not been advertised or subject to open competition, and that indirect discrimination had occurred. The court did not order any personal sanction against Harding; the finding is one against the appointment process.

Harding's husband, John Penrose, was Conservative MP for Weston-super-Mare from 2005 to 2024 and served as the Government's Anti-Corruption Champion from 2017. Reporting by openDemocracy notes that Penrose was also a member of the advisory board of the think tank 1828, which advocated replacing the NHS with an insurance model and scrapping Public Health England. Penrose has not been found by any regulator to have breached standards in connection with his wife's appointment. Gracchus carries this connection because the spousal-political overlap and the High Court's unlawfulness finding on the appointment process are both in the public record.

connections.summary: Gibraltar-registered Balshore Investments, described in YouGov's 2009 annual report as the 'family trust of Nadhim Zahawi', held more than 10% of YouGov - the polling firm Zahawi co-founded; HMRC's investigation concluded the resulting tax position involved a 'careless and not deliberate' error and Zahawi paid a settlement reported at c. £4.8m, after which Sir Laurie Magnus found Zahawi had committed a 'serious breach' of the Ministerial Code and he was dismissed as Conservative Party Chairman on 29 January 2023.
connections.detail:
Nadhim Zahawi co-founded YouGov plc and was its Chief Executive until entering Parliament in 2010. YouGov's 2009 annual report described Gibraltar-registered Balshore Investments, which then held more than 10% of YouGov, as the 'family trust of Nadhim Zahawi'. The founder shares were held by his father.

A tax investigation, opened initially by the National Crime Agency in 2020 and then transferred to HMRC, concluded that the tax treatment of the Balshore YouGov share sale involved an error that was 'careless and not deliberate'. Tax Policy Associates estimated the unpaid capital gains tax at c. £3.7m; Zahawi paid a settlement reported at c. £4.8m including a penalty.

In January 2023, Prime Minister Rishi Sunak ordered the Independent Adviser on Ministers' Interests, Sir Laurie Magnus, to investigate Zahawi's tax affairs and his disclosure of them while in office. Magnus's report found that Zahawi had committed a 'serious breach' of the Ministerial Code by failing to disclose the existence of the HMRC investigation when he became Chancellor in July 2022 and on subsequent occasions. Zahawi was dismissed as Conservative Party Chairman and Minister without Portfolio on 29 January 2023.

YouGov has held UK government research contracts with Cabinet Office, FCDO, DCMS and others, but is not a Gracchus-tracked prime supplier on the core 116 projects. Gracchus carries this case for the documented family-trust arrangement around a UK-government supplier and for the Independent Adviser's Ministerial Code finding.

connections.summary: Meller Designs Ltd, co-owned by Conservative donor David Meller, was awarded c. £164m of PPE contracts without competitive tender after referral via the 'VIP / high-priority lane' by Cabinet Office Minister Michael Gove; the High Court subsequently ruled the operation of the VIP lane unlawful, and Meller has admitted deleting emails relevant to the referral.
connections.detail:
Meller Designs Ltd, normally a fashion / homeware manufacturer, was awarded six PPE contracts during the COVID-19 pandemic worth a reported c. £164m. The contracts were awarded without competitive tender after the firm was routed through the Government's 'VIP / high-priority lane' following a referral by Cabinet Office Minister Michael Gove. Meller is a long-standing Conservative donor, with reported donations of more than £63,000 to the Conservative Party since 2009, and a former trustee of Policy Exchange.

In 2022 the High Court ruled, on a judicial review brought by the Good Law Project, that the operation of the VIP lane was unlawful: it had treated VIP-lane referrals preferentially in breach of the duty of equal treatment of suppliers under public-procurement law. The judgment is one against the operation of the lane rather than against any individual firm. Meller Designs reportedly made £13m profit after tax in 2020, and analysis indicated three of its six PPE contracts were priced above the average for comparable items.

In February 2024, reporting by Byline Times disclosed that Meller had admitted deleting emails relevant to the VIP-lane referral. As of early 2026 there is no criminal investigation in the public record against Meller personally. Meller Designs is not on Gracchus's canonical tracked-suppliers list (DHSC is the tracked buyer); the firm is carried as adjacent. Gracchus reports only the documented public-record findings.

connections.summary: Scott Benton, Conservative MP for Blackpool South and chair of the All-Party Parliamentary Group on Betting and Gaming, was filmed by an undercover Times investigation offering to lobby ministers, table parliamentary questions and leak a confidential gambling white paper containing market-sensitive information in exchange for £2,000-£4,000 a month from what he believed to be a fictitious gambling investment fund; the Commons Standards Committee recommended a 35-day suspension for an 'extremely serious breach' of the rules and Benton resigned as MP on 25 March 2024.
connections.detail:
In April 2023 The Times published the results of an undercover investigation in which Scott Benton, then Conservative MP for Blackpool South and chair of the APPG on Betting and Gaming, was filmed offering to lobby ministers, table parliamentary questions, and pass a confidential pre-publication copy of the Government's gambling white paper to what he believed to be a gambling investment fund called 'Tahr Partners'. The 'fund' was a fictitious entity created by Times reporters. Benton was filmed agreeing to retainers in the range of £2,000 to £4,000 per month.

The Commons Standards Committee subsequently found that Benton had committed an 'extremely serious breach' of the rules and recommended a 35-day suspension - one of the longest suspensions ever recommended by the Committee. Benton appealed and lost the appeal in February 2024. A recall petition was opened in his Blackpool South constituency. Benton resigned as MP on 25 March 2024, triggering a by-election.

There is no Gracchus-tracked prime supplier directly involved (DCMS gambling policy sits within the tracked policy perimeter but the gambling industry is not a Gracchus core-tracker sector). The case is carried as an adjacent record because the Standards Committee finding is one of the most definitive recent regulatory rulings on cash-for-access misconduct.

connections.summary: The William Cook group donated approximately £1.27m to the Conservative Party between 2005 and 2016 while its defence subsidiary Cook Defence Systems held UK Ministry of Defence track and running-gear contracts.
connections.detail:
Electoral Commission records show 10 reportable donations totalling £1,273,050 from the William Cook group of companies between 12 May 2005 and 10 February 2016. All 10 donations were to the Conservative Party. The donor name appears in three spelling variants in the Electoral Commission record (William Cook Holdings Limited, William Cook Holdings Ltd, William Cook Ltd) covering Companies House numbers 03283010 and 00074837.

The group's defence subsidiary, Cook Defence Systems, is a tracked supplier on Gracchus with £125m of disclosed contract value via Defence Equipment & Support (the procurement arm of the Ministry of Defence) for tracks and running gear used on UK armoured vehicle programmes. The supplier is single-buyer dependent (100% of tracked revenue from MoD).

The pattern here is single-party loyalty rather than cross-party hedging: every Electoral Commission record across an eleven-year window goes to the Conservative Party, while the firm's primary public-sector customer is the Ministry of Defence. Gracchus reports the figures and the concurrence; we do not assert that contracts were awarded in exchange for donations.

financial.relatedContractsDescription: £125m in tracked UK government contracts via Defence Equipment & Support (MoD)

connections.summary: Donated £77,500 to the Conservative Party in November 2009 while named as a contractor on six tracked UK government infrastructure programmes including Hinkley Point C, the 2012 Olympic Stadium and Ministry of Defence works.
connections.detail:
Electoral Commission records show one reportable donation of £77,500 from Sir Robert McAlpine Ltd (Companies House 00566823) to the Conservative Party on 17 November 2009.

Sir Robert McAlpine Limited is a tracked supplier on Gracchus across six buyers — NNB Generation Company (Hinkley Point C), the Ministry of Defence, the New Millennium Experience Company (Millennium Dome), the Olympic Delivery Authority (London 2012 Stadium), NHS England, and Rolls-Royce client board work. The contract values across these programmes are recorded as undisclosed in current source data, so the cumulative tracked-contract figure is not stated; the supplier appears on six separate publicly-tracked UK government programmes.

The pattern is single-party loyalty (one donation, Conservative) alongside a wide spread of tracked UK government contractor relationships across multiple departments and arm's-length bodies.

connections.summary: Heathrow Airport Holdings Limited donated £36,000 to Reform UK on 2 September 2025 while the Heathrow third-runway expansion project sits within DfT's Airports National Policy Statement programme.
connections.detail:
Electoral Commission records show one reportable donation of £36,000 from Heathrow Airport Holdings Limited (Companies House 05757208) to Reform UK on 2 September 2025.

Heathrow Airport Limited is a tracked counterparty on Gracchus via the Heathrow third-runway expansion programme, which is sponsored by the Department for Transport under the Airports National Policy Statement; the figures captured to date in the project record reflect litigation-cost references rather than primary works values, and the substantive expansion contracts are pending.

This case is the editorial outlier in the donor-contractor set. The other firms in this batch donate either to the party currently in government or to a long-running party of government — a pattern consistent with maintaining access to existing procurement relationships. Heathrow's recipient is Reform UK, which holds no UK government contracts and is not currently a party of government. Gracchus flags this for editorial visibility; we do not characterise the donor's intent.

connections.summary: Bamford-family corporate vehicles (JCB Research, JCB Service, JC Bamford Excavators, J.S. Bloor) and named family members donated approximately £19.15m to UK political parties between 2003 and 2025 - of which £17.33m to the Conservative Party - while JCB held UK Ministry of Defence contracts for military backhoe loaders and wheel loading shovels.
connections.detail:
Electoral Commission records show 70 reportable donations totalling £19,152,607 from the Bamford-family corporate cluster between 7 July 2003 and 3 November 2025. The donor identifiers split across at least 21 spelling variants (J C Bamford Excavators Ltd, JC Bamford Excavators Limited, J.C.B. Services, JCB Research, JCB Sales Ltd, J.S. Bloor (Services) Ltd, Bloor Holdings Ltd, plus named individuals Lord Anthony P Bamford and Mark J C Bamford). £17,332,860 was received by the Conservative Party, £200,000 by Reform UK and £1,619,747 logged as 'Other' (which on cross-reference with Electoral Commission categorisations is dominated by referendum / Brexit-aligned campaign vehicles).

In the same period JCB held UK Ministry of Defence contracts. The most-cited example is the 2010-11 British Army order for 138 4CX backhoe loaders and 98 436eHT wheel loading shovels, described in trade press as 'the largest order for machines for the British Army in more than 25 years'. JCB's defence-products division (HMEE - High Mobility Engineer Excavator, military backhoes, MOTS variants) supplies the British Army, MoD construction estate and overseas defence customers. Separately, in November 2022 the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy announced £26m of public funding for a JCB hydrogen-engine project; the announcement was made by then-Business Secretary while JCB-linked donations were continuing.

JCB is not on the Gracchus tracker's list of 116 monitored programmes (it is not a prime contractor on any of the major outsourced public-service projects we follow). The connection is therefore carried as adjacent_firm rather than as a tracked supplier. Gracchus reports the Electoral Commission figures and the publicly-disclosed contracts; we do not assert that contracts were awarded as a quid pro quo for donations.

counterparty.note: JCB is a long-running supplier to the British Army (military backhoe loaders, wheel loading shovels, HMEE) but is not a Gracchus-tracked prime contractor on the 116 monitored programmes. Carried as adjacent_firm.

connections.summary: Boris Johnson signed as Daily Mail columnist in June 2023 - submitting his ACOBA application 30 minutes before the Mail tweeted the announcement - which the Advisory Committee on Business Appointments ruled 'a clear and unambiguous breach of the Rules and the requirements set out in the Ministerial Code'. He subsequently took a paid GB News presenting role (October 2023), which ACOBA approved subject to conditions.
connections.detail:
Johnson left office as Prime Minister in September 2022 and as MP for Uxbridge in June 2023. On 16 June 2023 his office submitted an ACOBA application for a Daily Mail columnist role at 12.31pm; the Daily Mail's Twitter account announced the appointment 30 minutes later, at 1.01pm. ACOBA Chair Lord Pickles wrote to Johnson and to then-Deputy Prime Minister Oliver Dowden the same week confirming a breach of the Business Appointment Rules and the Ministerial Code, and declined to give advice on the appointment. Press reports (ITV News, Press Gazette) put the column's reported value at around £1m a year, though ACOBA correspondence does not state remuneration.

In October 2023 Johnson sought ACOBA advice for a separate paid role as 'Presenter and Contributor' at GB News. ACOBA approved that appointment subject to standard conditions: a two-year ban on lobbying the UK government on behalf of GB News, a bar on advising on UK-government bids or contracts during that window, and the standard restriction on drawing on privileged information. The Cabinet Office told ACOBA that, given two administrations had passed since Johnson's time in office, his knowledge was 'unlikely to offer any unfair advantage to GB News'.

Johnson has subsequently been the subject of further ACOBA breach correspondence in connection with Merlyn Advisors Ltd. The Cabinet Office took no enforcement action over the Daily Mail breach; then-Deputy PM Dowden indicated to Parliament that any sanction would be 'disproportionate', and signalled a future reform direction in which media work would be exempted from the Business Appointment Rules.

counterparty.note: Neither Associated Newspapers nor GB News is a Gracchus-tracked prime contractor. Carried as adjacent_firm for the post-office appointment record.

regulatoryFindings.quotedText: The announcement of your column in the Daily Mail was a clear and unambiguous breach of the Rules and the requirements set out in the Ministerial Code - both of which you are familiar with. ACOBA's established practice is not to provide advice in these circumstances.
regulatoryFindings.outcome: Breach of the Business Appointment Rules and Ministerial Code recorded; ACOBA declined to give advice. No further enforcement action by the Cabinet Office.

connections.summary: Sir Iain Duncan Smith co-founded the Centre for Social Justice (CSJ) in 2004 and resumed its chairmanship after resigning from the Cabinet in 2016; CSJ research formed the policy basis for Universal Credit and the Work Programme, both of which he subsequently delivered as Secretary of State for Work and Pensions (2010-16).
connections.detail:
The Centre for Social Justice is a centre-right think tank co-founded in 2004 by Duncan Smith with Tim Montgomerie and Philippa Stroud. It published the 2009 paper 'Dynamic Benefits' that proposed the Universal Credit single-payment model. From May 2010 to March 2016 Duncan Smith served as Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, with departmental responsibility for designing and rolling out Universal Credit, the Work Programme and pension reform - policy briefs whose intellectual lineage was traced back, by his department's own statements, to CSJ research.

Duncan Smith resigned from the Cabinet in March 2016 in protest at planned cuts to disability benefits, and resumed the chairmanship of CSJ. The dual role - sitting MP plus chair of a think tank that publishes policy proposals on welfare, family policy and criminal justice - is recorded openly in the Register of Members' Financial Interests. The Register entry for 18 March 2024 lists, under category 3 (Gifts, benefits and hospitality from UK sources): 'Name of donor: Centre for Social Justice (CSJ) ... Associated with visiting various charities/organisations in Scotland in my capacity as Chairman of the CSJ, flights, accommodation and other transport related costs with a total value of £3,370 ... Date received: 25-27 May 2023 ... Donor status: company, registration 05137036'.

No regulator or court has made any finding of rule-breaking; the dual role is properly disclosed and predates Duncan Smith's most recent Cabinet appointments. The pattern of interest is structural rather than transactional: CSJ proposals shaped the welfare-reform agenda Duncan Smith later implemented from inside government, and CSJ continues to lobby successive governments (including the post-2024 Labour administration, per The Canary's 2024 reporting) on the welfare brief he chaired. Carried as a dual_role record so the connection is searchable from Duncan Smith's MP profile and from any future tracker entry on welfare-policy contracts.

connections.summary: Sir John Whittingdale took multiple paid post-office appointments after leaving DCMS in July 2016 - Authors Licensing and Collecting Society, SWNS Media Group, the Federation for International Polo, and (without ACOBA permission) a £1,000-an-hour Alphasights consulting role on the Betting and Gaming Council brief - several of which sat in policy areas he had directly regulated.
connections.detail:
Whittingdale was Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport from May 2015 to July 2016, with portfolio responsibility for press regulation, broadcasting (including audio-visual policy and the BBC charter review), gambling, and creative-industries copyright. After leaving Cabinet he sought ACOBA advice for paid roles including: SWNS Media Group (advisory board, c.1 day a month, advising on developments in the newspaper, audio-visual and online media sectors); Authors Licensing and Collecting Society (ALCS, regulatory and policy advice from April 2017, paid £2,000 a quarter from October 2018); and the Federation for International Polo (FIP) as a paid part-time consultant.

In 2022 openDemocracy and Business Insider reported that Whittingdale had taken a £1,000-an-hour consulting role with Alphasights, advising on the Betting and Gaming Council brief, without first seeking ACOBA permission. ACOBA published correspondence on Alphasights and the Betting and Gaming Council (linked above) recording the failure to seek prior advice. Whittingdale returned to government as Minister of State for Media and Data (2020-22) under Boris Johnson, with policy oversight again of the broadcasting and online-media sectors he had advised privately.

No court finding has been made. The pattern of interest is sector-overlap: every disclosed post-Cabinet role sat in the broadcasting, press, copyright or gambling-policy areas Whittingdale had departmental responsibility for as Culture Secretary, and the Alphasights/Betting and Gaming Council engagement was the subject of ACOBA correspondence after the fact. Carried as post_office_appointment with status closed_with_finding to reflect the ACOBA correspondence on the Alphasights breach.
